meta.description: Australian candidate transparency dataset: the federal 48th Parliament plus state/territory parliaments (see jurisdiction field). Federal roster from AEC Members Elected (House) and OpenAustralia (Senate) with AEC donor data; state rosters from data/sources/states/. Policy positions are added separately with their own sources. See ../CONTRIBUTING.md.

immigration.summary: Payne criticises Australia's immigration detention as 'destructive and expensive' and argues indefinite detention is unjustifiable. She supports abolishing temporary protection visas, expanding the government-funded humanitarian intake (citing Labor's 27,000-place commitment) plus a 5,000-place community-sponsored program, increasing UNHCR funding, and accepting New Zealand's offer to resettle refugees.

sources.title: Refugees - 23/06/2021

foreign_policy.summary: In a 28 July 2025 interview, Payne said she has continually called for an immediate and permanent ceasefire in Gaza as the most effective way to protect innocent lives, stated that by not allowing aid into Gaza Israel is breaching international law, and said she longs to see Australia recognise a Palestinian state as soon as possible, at a time most advantageous to peace.

sources.title: Afternoon Briefing Panel 28th July 2025

faith.summary: Andrew Leigh is a self-described atheist; he has written and spoken publicly about being an atheist while arguing that churches benefit society.

immigration.summary: Leigh, a Labor MP and economist, publicly defends immigration, stating in 2026 that the government has 'reduced migration from the record highs it was under the Coalition.' He has more broadly framed Australia's non-discriminatory and skilled migration system as a source of national strength.

sources.title: Andrew Leigh can't stop lying on immigration

foreign_policy.summary: Leigh has advocated for evidence-based foreign aid, stating the Australian Government understands its obligation to ensure aid delivered has the maximum positive impact and arguing that randomised trials and rigorous evaluation should determine which development interventions work.

sources.title: The call for evidence-based development

economic_nationalism.summary: Andrew Leigh is a strong advocate for free trade and unilateral tariff reduction, opposing protectionism. He has argued Australia should continue advocating for tariff removal without imposing retaliatory tariffs, stating "Australia is a trading nation and a strong advocate for open and rules-based trade" and "Trade expands opportunity. Isolation shrinks it."

sources.title: Leigh takes a swipe at US tariffs with passionate speech in defence of free trade

immigration.summary: Hawke, a former Liberal Immigration Minister, has called for the government to resolve the status of roughly 7,500 long-term asylum seekers, saying: 'I think the Government should resolve this now. People have been here a long time and the reality of their condition is that they will be staying here.'

foreign_policy.summary: As Minister for International Development and the Pacific, Hawke called for closer ties to and development assistance for the Pacific region, stating 'their prosperity is our prosperity,' while supporting a reduction in the overall foreign aid budget. He also argued his Pacific engagement did not require further action by Australia on climate change.

economic_nationalism.summary: In a 2019 keynote, Alex Hawke referenced the Coalition government's legislation "establishing the kind of rules around foreign investment in critical national infrastructure" and "banning foreign donations into the Australian political system," stating this legislation "has really shored up the ability of Australia to stand strong" and that "a strong, secure sovereign region fundamentally enables economic prosperity."

sources.title: Australia's Focus on the Pacific - Hon Alex Hawke MP's Keynote Speech to the AIIA National Conference

citizenship_eligibility.summary: During the 2017 citizenship questions over members with foreign-born parents, Hawke stated he is 'an Australian citizen only and have never held or acquired or sought Greek or any other citizenship.' He was not referred to the High Court.

faith.summary: Anthony Albanese describes his Catholic faith as part of who he is, citing his Catholic upbringing as an influence on his values; he is generally described as a non-practising Catholic and was sworn in by affirmation rather than on a Bible.

sources.title: 'It's a part of who I am': Albanese on his Catholic faith

immigration.summary: As Prime Minister, Albanese's government in February 2023 abolished temporary protection visas, allowing up to 19,000 asylum seekers to stay in Australia permanently. In his 1996 maiden speech he expressed support for multiculturalism.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

foreign_policy.summary: As Prime Minister, Albanese's government formally recognised the State of Palestine (announced August 2025, effective 21 September 2025) as part of a coordinated push for a two-state solution; he officially commenced the AUKUS security pact on 13 March 2023 (acquiring nuclear-powered submarines); restored high-level dialogue with China, meeting Xi Jinping in November 2022 and visiting China in November 2023; and pledged a further $100 million in aid to Ukraine in July 2022.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

economic_nationalism.summary: Anthony Albanese has championed the "Future Made in Australia" agenda, stating "Our Government is investing in manufacturing to make more things here" and that government must "be more assertive in capitalising on our comparative advantages and building sovereign capability in areas of national interest." He cited "a new and widespread willingness to make economic interventions on the basis of national interest and national sovereignty."

sources.title: A future made in Australia

citizenship_eligibility.summary: Although birth to an Italian father would ordinarily confer Italian citizenship, his father was not recorded on his birth certificate, so he was not a dual citizen and met the eligibility requirements of section 44 of the Constitution.

sources.title: Anthony Albanese

sources.title: Wikipedia — Barnaby Joyce

immigration.summary: In September 2015 Joyce called for Australia to accept more Syrian refugees, with a preference to prioritise Christian refugees. In 2016 he opposed One Nation's proposed Muslim immigration ban, and he has stated his opposition to banning the burqa.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce

foreign_policy.summary: On 10 August 2018, Joyce defended maintaining Australia's foreign aid budget, framing aid to Pacific island nations as a strategic necessity, saying Australia needs to support island nations 'otherwise the Chinese government will just move in there and support them.'

sources.title: Joyce defends foreign aid against farmers' criticism

economic_nationalism.summary: Barnaby Joyce has stated a preference for Australian ownership of agricultural assets, saying "My preference is always for the Australian people to be owner of the Australian asset" and "I'll never work to the exclusion of foreign investment but my preference is always for the Australian owner of the Australian asset." He supported blocking selected foreign bids (e.g. Kidman & Co) and warned against monopolistic foreign control of agricultural infrastructure.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce's foreign investment odyssey

citizenship_eligibility.summary: Held New Zealand citizenship by descent from his father, which the High Court found rendered him ineligible under section 44(i); his 2016 election was declared invalid on 27 October 2017. Having renounced his New Zealand citizenship in August 2017, he was eligible to recontest and won the December 2017 New England by-election.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce

immigration.summary: As Minister for Immigration and Citizenship (2010-2013), Bowen secured an offshore processing arrangement with Malaysia (the "Malaysia Solution") in 2011, under which Australia would transfer asylum seekers who arrived by boat to Malaysia in exchange for resettling refugees from there; the arrangement was struck down by the High Court in September 2011.

sources.title: Chris Bowen: Israel can only be free when Palestine is
